item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why? They don't know how the anomaly works, it's just that it doesn't do anything anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

How did the Foundation suspect the anomaly before the alteration?

Because it was a photo made by a Group of Interest that is watched by the Foundation because of anomalous activity, of course. It’s a good idea to at least investigate the photo instead of letting it off as "oh he was just taking a photo" and then the photo actually turns out to be anomalous and opens a sinkhole in NYC.

Why it's an SCP and not an anomalous item or even just extranormal event?

As stated in the article, the item is pending another reclassification, because based off the chatlogs and the item’s activity supporting the chatlogs, they’re willing to consider that they goofed.

Which happened once, and unlikely to happen in the future

Evident to the reader, but why would the Foundation come to such a conclusion so easily? Why would the Foundation be as quick to believe a GOI (this being one that is built upon fucking around) as a wiki browser would?

By the way, aren't kkrule's anomalous abilities too overpowered, so they can change or delete information about themself?

To "change or delete information about themself" would definitely be quite a broad ability, sure, but kkrule here was changing a photo, not his genetic makeup.

I think that the Foundation should research the anomalous information alteration and protection measures instead of containing the non-anomalous picture

That would probably work out better, considering what I intended for this item to be, but again, the Foundation doesn’t know its non-anomalous. Taking risks and jumping to such conclusions with things that defy the laws of nature is, well, y’know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How did the Foundation suspect the anomaly before the alteration? Why it's an SCP and not an anomalous item or even just extranormal event?

SCP-4816 was previously capable of altering its contents,

Which happened once, and unlikely to happen in the future. SCP-4816 changed not because of its anomalous properties, but because of actions of kkrule. By the way, aren't kkrule's anomalous abilities too overpowered, so they can change or delete information about themself? I think that the Foundation should research the anomalous information alteration and protection measures instead of containing the non-anomalous picture (there's SCP-3936, but its protection happened to be too effective).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Change neutralized to explained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: aw, this is sweet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels pretty lacking. I think it’s short length works to it’s detriment. Feels like the punchline is in the middle of the article, and then the rest is too short to get into really interestinf territory. So, downvote from me.

item.title: Re: What
item.content:encoded: it's him irl. YOU HAVE TO PAY ATTENTION

item.title: COOL!
item.content:encoded: this is a really good story i like it!

item.title: Re: What
item.content:encoded: The door

item.title: What
item.content:encoded: A door?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fun and heartwarming and perfectly represents why I enjoy GAW articles. Not everything needs to be some big, flashy, XK Scenario. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Said apps don't let you change every instance of that image, including caches, screenshots, pictures, and reproductions of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dont get why its an SCP. He just changed the picture didnt he? Many messaging apps let you edit messages you sent, you could just add a different image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
GAW always makes me smile when they do their IRC interactions, because you can see pretty much very IRC community in miniature right there, their scips reflect back on this, they're not malevolent, or -K-Class or even really douchey.
They just come across as innocents mostly, doing stuff because it's funny or because they want to troll the Man, in strong contrast to the immature and cynical nihilism of AWCY, GAW aren't pretentious, they're having fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like gaw is at its best when really exploring their characters. I was interested in where this was going to head initially, but ended up seeming vapid and unfulfilling.

There's nothing really interesting about the item (it's about as vanilla as they come), so I can't really even appreciate it for what it is.

The final interaction, given that any history or buildup is relayed to an afterthought of a footnote, falls completely flat; making it feel inauthentic and ultimately meaningless.

This especially, feels like its getting around characterization via tell over show:

Thanks so much for looking out for me!! i know ur always mean to me in chat but you really are a good caring person in the heart :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: a really sweet story!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: pretty cute

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's definitely a valid point. I felt like the punchline that'd come with putting it in the chat log was worth including though, even if it sacrificed in-universe logic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's driving me crazy here is that there's not a pic with the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me - one of the things I like about GAW is that I can imagine them associating with my son (17, currently non-anomalous*). He would definitely be DonDeLillo in this situation.

*As far as I know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good when a GAW SCP realizes that, while the group is a bunch of memers, they do try to be very nice and kind to each other. Like a big family. Even when one of their member is a little slow on stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Item Tag” is a headcanonical thingy. A few people have pointed this out across my articles, which is interesting to me; Is “Item #” a weirder thing to change than “Object Class”? It’s fun to think about.

Thanks for the upvote! Here’s to hoping others feel the same way as you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel a bit odd upvoting this since it's a vastly simple object and story, but I couldn't help but be entertained by it. It's super sweet as well, and the community feel that you're emphasizing here is a good way to bring out depth. Great job on such a short article with addena being real good and touching.

Is Item Tag over Item # on purpose? I ask because I almost changed that on instinct.

item.title: Author Post >:^|
item.content:encoded:
I'm really glad this was the number used to start off my 4k articles, specifically because:

4816
4 x 2 = 8
8 x 2 = 16
4, 8, 16

What a fun lil' number (to me at least)! Anyways, I definitely doubted this article’s success throughout the critting and drafting process, so I'm glad people are seeing this for what I wanted it to be. The image was made by me btw, in my smol bathroom.

Critter Credits:

Zyn
not_a_seagull
Lazar Lyusternik

… and several other users whom I do not remember currently. Let me know if you gave feedback at some point!

(author page pls subscrib)

May this not be downvoted into oblivion!
